title: Finding the Balance Point (Nash Equilibrium)
concept: An equilibrium is a balance point where neither the generator nor the discriminator can improve further by changing their own move alone — like two rival shops settling into stable prices.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In the GAN minimax objective, what does the generator try to minimize?

options.text: The discriminator's accuracy at spotting its fakes
The number of images it generates

exercises.explain: Right! The generator pulls the discriminator's accuracy down. But does this tug-of-war go on forever?

cards.title: Does the tug-of-war ever stop?
cards.body: Every round, the generator tweaks its fakes and the discriminator tweaks its judgments. It's natural to wonder — does this back-and-forth just continue forever, or does it settle somewhere?

cards.title: What is a balance point?
cards.body: A balance point (sometimes called an equilibrium) is a state where neither side can do any better by changing only their own move, given what the other side is currently doing. From the outside, things look steady — even though both sides are still fully capable of acting.

cards.title: Two coffee shops across the street
cards.body: Imagine two rival coffee shops facing each other. If one drops its price, customers flow over — so the other matches the drop. If one raises its price, it loses customers — so it backs off. Eventually both shops settle on prices where neither gains anything by changing alone. That settled state is the balance point.

cards.title: Approaching the balance point
cards.body: Early in GAN training, the discriminator easily out-scores the generator, and the gap swings wildly round to round. As training continues, each side's changes start producing smaller and smaller shifts in the score — a sign they're approaching a balance point, just like the coffee shops' prices settling down.

cards.title: Balance doesn't mean 'giving up'
cards.body: You might think a balance point means both sides stop trying. In reality, both the generator and discriminator are still fully active — it's just that, given the other's current move, changing their own move alone wouldn't help them anymore.

cards.title: Before vs. at the balance point
cards.body: Comparing the two stages of training:

left.text:
Scores swing widely
round to round
One side clearly ahead

right.text:
Scores barely shift
round to round
Neither side gains by moving alone

exercises.prompt: What best describes a balance point (equilibrium) between two competing sides?

options.text: A state where neither side can improve further by changing only their own move
A state where one side has completely defeated the other
A state where both sides stop making any moves ever again
A state that only happens in games with more than two players

exercises.explain: A balance point is about neither side gaining from a solo change — not about anyone quitting or winning outright.

exercises.prompt: True or false: At a balance point, both sides are still capable of acting, they just wouldn't gain anything by changing their move alone.
exercises.explain: True! Balance doesn't mean inactivity — it means solo changes stop paying off.

exercises.sentence: Two rival coffee shops settling into stable prices, where neither gains by changing alone, is an example of a ___ point.
exercises.explain: That settled, stable state — where neither side benefits from changing alone — is the balance point.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is the clearest sign that two competing systems have reached a balance point?

options.text: Round-to-round changes in the score become small and steady instead of swinging wildly
The score keeps swinging more wildly every round

exercises.explain: Small, steady round-to-round changes signal that neither side benefits from moving alone anymore — that's a balance point.

exercises.prompt: Two food trucks parked near each other keep adjusting their taco prices every day. After a few weeks, both trucks charge $4 and stop changing their price, since raising or lowering it alone would lose them customers or profit. What is this a real-world example of?

options.text: A balance point (equilibrium) — neither truck can improve further by changing its own price alone
One truck permanently defeating the other
Proof that the trucks have stopped operating

exercises.explain: Exactly — that settled $4-vs-$4 standoff is a balance point. GANs are aiming for their own version of this settled state. Next: what does that ideal balance point actually look like for a generator and discriminator?
